title: Stator failure — chronic undercharging
description: Harley stators are a known weak point across all EFI models. The stator windings break down from heat, causing progressive charging failure. Bike may run fine but battery slowly dies. Often misdiagnosed as a bad battery.
causes: Stator winding insulation breakdown from heat
fix_procedure: 1. Test stator AC output at connector (disconnect regulator): should read 18-22V AC per leg at 2000 RPM. 2. Check for shorts to ground on each stator leg (should be open/infinite). 3. If low output or shorted, replace stator. 4. While in there, inspect rotor for demagnetized spots. 5. Replace voltage regulator if stator connector shows heat damage. 6. Forum tip: upgrade to a Cycle Electric stator — runs cooler than OEM.

title: Cam chain tensioner failure — Twin Cam tick of death
description: Early Twin Cam 88 engines used spring-loaded hydraulic cam chain tensioners with plastic shoes that wear through. When the shoe wears to nothing, chain contacts tensioner body metal-on-metal. Known as the 'tick of death' — if ignored, chain can skip and destroy the engine. Forum consensus: every TC88 should get upgraded tensioners as preventive maintenance.
fix_procedure: 1. Remove cam cover and inspect tensioner shoes — any visible metal = immediate replacement. 2. Replace with hydraulic tensioners with metal shoes (S&S, Feuling, or Wood Performance). 3. Replace cam chain while in there. 4. Check cam bearing play. 5. Forum tip: do NOT just replace shoes — upgrade the entire tensioner to a gear-driven cam setup if budget allows (eliminates the problem permanently).
parts_needed: Upgraded cam chain tensioners (S&S 330-0519 or Feuling 8080)

title: Compensator sprocket noise — primary clunk on startup
description: The compensator sprocket absorbs drivetrain shock between engine and transmission. When the internal spring pack wears, it produces a loud clunk/rattle on startup and at low RPM. Extremely common on Twin Cam and Milwaukee-Eight models. Often confused with cam chain noise.
fix_procedure: 1. Confirm diagnosis: clunk on startup that goes away once running, returns on decel/accel transitions. 2. Remove primary cover, inspect compensator for play. 3. Replace with SE Screamin Eagle compensator or Baker Buddy — both eliminate the problem. 4. Forum tip: while primary is open, inspect primary chain tensioner and clutch pack.

title: Intake manifold air leak — lean running and backfire
description: The rubber intake manifold seals between the cylinder heads and throttle body/carburetor harden and crack with heat cycles. Causes a vacuum leak that leans out the mixture, especially at idle. Extremely common across all Harley V-twins. The #1 cause of lean-related codes and backfiring on decel.
fix_procedure: 1. Diagnose: spray carb cleaner around intake seals while running — RPM increase confirms leak. 2. Remove throttle body/carb. 3. Replace both intake seals (always do both). 4. Use new clamps — old ones lose tension. 5. Torque to spec (don't overtighten or seals extrude). 6. Forum tip: use James Gasket seals (JGI-27062-95) — much better than OEM. Apply a thin coat of Hylomar to the seal surfaces.
parts_needed: Intake manifold seals x2 (James Gasket JGI-27062-95)

title: Voltage regulator failure — overcharging or no charge
description: Harley voltage regulators fail in two modes: undercharging (battery dies) or overcharging (boils battery, fries electronics). Overcharging is more dangerous — can destroy the battery and damage the ECM. Often fails together with the stator. Forum wisdom: always replace reg and stator together.
fix_procedure: 1. Test: battery voltage should be 13.5-14.5V at 2000 RPM. Below 13V = undercharge. Above 15V = overcharge. 2. If overcharging: disconnect immediately to prevent battery damage. 3. Replace voltage regulator. 4. Test stator before buttoning up — bad stator kills new regulators. 5. Forum tip: upgrade to a MOSFET-style regulator (like a Compu-Fire or Ricks) — runs cooler and lasts longer than OEM. Mount with thermal paste for heat dissipation.

title: Sportster primary chain tensioner wear
description: Sportster primary chain tensioner shoe wears over time, causing chain slap and noise from the primary. Worse at idle, gets quieter as RPM rises. Not as catastrophic as cam chain issues but annoying and can damage primary components if left too long.
fix_procedure: 1. Check primary fluid level first — low fluid accelerates wear. 2. Remove primary inspection cover and check chain deflection (should be 5/8 to 7/8 inch cold). 3. If deflection is excessive with tensioner fully extended, replace shoe and/or chain. 4. Forum tip: some guys run the chain slightly tight rather than loose — less slap, but check more frequently.

title: Turn signal module failure — erratic blinkers
description: The turn signal security module (TSM/TSSM) handles turn signals, security system, and starter lockout. When it fails, turn signals may blink fast, not blink, or blink on their own. Security system may also lock you out of starting. Common on 2004-2013 models.
fix_procedure: 1. Check for stored codes with scan tool. 2. Inspect TSSM connector under the seat for corrosion or water. 3. Try re-seating connector first. 4. If module is confirmed bad, replace. Must be programmed to the bike's key fobs. 5. Forum tip: before buying a new TSSM, check the ground wire on the left side under the tank — a bad ground causes identical symptoms and is a $0 fix.
parts_needed: Key fob batteries (while you're at it)

title: Exhaust header leak — ticking when cold
description: Exhaust header pipe gaskets and flange connections develop leaks, especially on the front cylinder which gets more thermal cycling. Sounds like a tick or hiss when cold, usually goes away as the pipes expand when hot. Often confused with valve train noise.
causes: Loose header bolts (thermal cycling loosens them)
fix_procedure: 1. Confirm: listen at each header-to-head joint when cold. Tick that fades as bike warms = exhaust leak. 2. Re-torque header bolts to spec. 3. If still leaking, pull headers and replace exhaust gaskets. 4. Inspect header flange faces for warping. 5. Forum tip: use Cometic gaskets and anti-seize on the bolts. Re-torque after 2-3 heat cycles.

title: Fuel injector clogging — lean stumble off idle
description: Fuel injectors get gummed up from ethanol fuel and short trips. Causes a lean stumble or hesitation when cracking the throttle off idle. More common on bikes that sit for weeks between rides. Often misdiagnosed as a TPS issue.
fix_procedure: 1. Try fuel injector cleaner additive first (Chevron Techron). 2. If no improvement after 2 tanks, remove injectors and send for ultrasonic cleaning or replace. 3. Check fuel pressure (should be ~55-62 PSI). 4. Inspect fuel filter/screen in tank. 5. Forum tip: run non-ethanol fuel if available in your area. Add StaBil if bike sits more than 2 weeks. Always run the bike until the carbs/injectors are warm before shutting down.

title: Rear brake switch failure — brake light always on or never on
description: The rear brake light switch (hydraulic pressure type) fails, causing the brake light to stay on constantly or not come on at all. A constant-on brake light drains the battery overnight. Common across all models with hydraulic rear brakes.
fix_procedure: 1. Check brake light operation — stuck on or never on. 2. Disconnect switch at connector — if light goes off, switch is stuck closed. 3. Replace brake light switch. 4. Bleed rear brake after replacement (switch is in the hydraulic circuit). 5. Forum tip: the OEM switch is cheap — don't bother trying to fix it. Keep a spare in your saddlebag.
